title: Top 7 Earnings Surprises of Q1 2026
metaDescription: Discover the top 7 earnings surprises of Q1 2026. From stunning beats to shocking misses, explore the companies that defied expectations and moved markets this earnings season.
excerpt: The Q1 2026 earnings season delivered its share of stunners. From Palantir's AI-fueled blowout to UnitedHealth's shocking miss, here are the seven earnings surprises that left Wall Street reeling.

content.introduction:
Every earnings season produces its share of surprises, but Q1 2026 delivered more than the usual quotient of shockers. In a quarter defined by the acceleration of artificial intelligence investment, a gradual softening of the consumer economy, and persistent uncertainty around Federal Reserve policy, several companies delivered results that dramatically exceeded or fell short of Wall Street expectations — and their stock prices moved accordingly. The average absolute stock price move on earnings report days reached 5.8% during Q1 2026, the highest level since Q1 2023, underscoring the heightened stakes for each quarterly report. When a company surprises to the upside in this environment, the rewards can be enormous; when it disappoints, the punishment is swift and severe.

Earnings surprises matter far beyond the immediate stock price reaction. A positive surprise often signals that a company's competitive position is strengthening, that management is executing more effectively than anticipated, or that end-market demand is more robust than the consensus assumed. Conversely, a negative surprise can reveal previously hidden vulnerabilities — whether in the form of margin compression, market share loss, or strategic missteps. For investors, these surprises provide critical information that can reshape assumptions about a company's future earnings power and, consequently, its intrinsic value. They also create opportunities: stocks that are unfairly punished by a negative surprise may present buying opportunities for investors who can distinguish between temporary setbacks and fundamental deterioration, while positive surprises can validate investment theses and provide confidence to increase positions.

The seven surprises highlighted below were selected based on the magnitude of the earnings deviation from consensus estimates, the size of the subsequent stock price move, the strategic significance of the underlying business developments, and the broader implications for the sectors and themes shaping the market in 2026. Together, they tell a compelling story about where the economy and corporate profits are heading — and where the biggest risks and opportunities lie.

items.name: Palantir Technologies (PLTR) — AI Juggernaut Obliterates Expectations
items.detail: Palantir delivered what may go down as the single most impressive earnings beat of the entire Q1 2026 season, reporting revenue of $883 million, a staggering 36% year-over-year increase that exceeded the Wall Street consensus by 14.2%. The company's US commercial revenue was the standout, surging 54% to $328 million as enterprises across healthcare, manufacturing, energy, and financial services rushed to deploy Palantir's AIP (Artificial Intelligence Platform) for operational decision-making. CEO Alex Karp described the demand environment as 'unlike anything we have seen in our 21-year history' and noted that the company closed 139 deals worth over $1 million during the quarter, up from 87 in Q1 2025. Government revenue grew 19% to $555 million, driven by expanding deployments with the Department of Defense and intelligence agencies, but the commercial segment's explosive growth is what truly captivated investors. Operating margins expanded to a record 26.8% from 18.2% a year ago, demonstrating powerful operating leverage as the company scales its platform with relatively modest incremental costs. Free cash flow reached $242 million for the quarter, and the company raised its full-year 2026 revenue guidance to $3.68 billion from $3.44 billion, implying approximately 31% growth. The stock surged 23% in the two days following the report, adding over $40 billion in market capitalization and cementing Palantir's status as one of the hottest AI plays in the market. Analysts scrambled to raise price targets, with the most bullish calling for $150 per share based on a projected 2027 revenue run rate exceeding $5 billion. Skeptics, however, cautioned that the stock's premium valuation — trading at over 60 times forward sales — leaves no room for execution missteps and that the company's customer concentration remains a risk, with its top 10 accounts representing approximately 35% of total revenue.

items.name: UnitedHealth Group (UNH) — Healthcare Giant's Stunning Miss
items.detail: UnitedHealth Group delivered the most negative earnings surprise of Q1 2026, reporting adjusted earnings per share of $6.91, missing the consensus estimate of $7.42 by a shocking 6.9% — the company's largest earnings miss in over a decade. The miss was driven by a dramatic and unexpected increase in the medical care ratio, which rose to 86.4% from 83.2% in Q1 2025 and well above the company's guided range of 83-84%. Management attributed the elevated utilization primarily to its Medicare Advantage business, where outpatient procedures, specialist visits, and costly specialty drug claims all exceeded actuarial projections. CEO Andrew Witty acknowledged that the company's pricing assumptions for 2026 Medicare Advantage plans, set during the 2025 bid cycle, had underestimated the pace at which post-pandemic utilization normalization would accelerate, particularly among the 65-and-older demographic that had deferred care during 2020-2023. The company also reported a $1.4 billion reserve charge to strengthen claims reserves, signaling that management expects the elevated utilization trend to persist. UnitedHealth cut its full-year 2026 adjusted EPS guidance to $28.25-$29.25 from $29.50-$30.00, a significant reduction that sent shockwaves through the managed care sector. The stock plummeted 14% on the day of the report, erasing approximately $55 billion in market value — the largest single-day market cap destruction for any stock during the Q1 earnings season. The ripple effects were immediate: Humana fell 9%, CVS Health declined 7%, and Cigna dropped 5%, as investors repriced the entire subsector on the assumption that UnitedHealth's utilization challenges were likely industry-wide rather than company-specific. The UnitedHealth surprise has fundamentally altered the investment thesis for health insurers, raising questions about whether the several-year tailwind from suppressed post-pandemic utilization has definitively ended and whether Medicare Advantage pricing will prove adequate in the current environment.

items.name: Eli Lilly (LLY) — GLP-1 Powerhouse Crushes Estimates Again
items.detail: Eli Lilly continued its remarkable run of earnings outperformance in Q1 2026, reporting revenue of $14.2 billion, up 32% year-over-year and exceeding consensus estimates by 7.8%. The beat was driven once again by the company's GLP-1 receptor agonist franchise, with Mounjaro generating $5.1 billion in revenue and Zepbound contributing $3.8 billion, both figures well above Street expectations. The combined GLP-1 revenue of $8.9 billion represented 63% of total company sales and grew 68% year-over-year, demonstrating that demand for these groundbreaking diabetes and obesity treatments continues to massively outstrip supply despite significant manufacturing capacity additions. CEO David Ricks announced that the company had resolved the supply constraints that had limited Zepbound availability since its launch, and that the company was now able to fill 94% of prescriptions within 48 hours, up from approximately 70% at the start of 2025. The company's newer incretin therapies, including the triple agonist retatrutide, showed promising Phase 3 trial results with average weight loss of 26.7% — surpassing both Zepbound's 22.5% and Novo Nordisk's CagriSema — positioning Lilly to further dominate the obesity treatment market if the drug receives approval as expected in early 2027. Operating margins expanded to 32.4% from 27.8% a year ago, reflecting the extraordinary pricing power and operating leverage of the GLP-1 franchise. Eli Lilly raised its full-year revenue guidance to $58.5-$60.5 billion from $56-$58 billion, implying approximately 28% growth at the midpoint. The stock rose 8.5% on the report, pushing the company's market capitalization above $950 billion and within striking distance of becoming the first pharmaceutical company to reach a $1 trillion valuation. Analysts noted that the obesity treatment market is still in its early innings, with an estimated 100 million Americans qualifying for GLP-1 therapy but only approximately 12 million currently prescribed, suggesting years of continued growth ahead.

items.name: Delta Air Lines (DAL) — Turbulence at 35,000 Feet
items.detail: Delta Air Lines delivered a shocking negative surprise in Q1 2026, reporting adjusted earnings per share of $0.36, missing the consensus estimate of $0.72 by a staggering 50% and sending the stock down 11% in a single session. The miss was driven by a combination of factors that painted a concerning picture for the airline industry. Unit revenue, as measured by passenger revenue per available seat mile (PRASM), declined 4.2% year-over-year, significantly worse than the 1.5% decline analysts had expected, as aggressive capacity additions across the industry outstripped demand growth. Domestic leisure demand softened notably, with management describing a 'measurable step down' in bookings from price-sensitive travelers during March as consumer confidence wavered. Corporate travel, which had been a bright spot, showed signs of plateauing, with managed corporate revenue growing just 1% after several quarters of 5-7% growth. Fuel costs were also a headwind, with jet fuel averaging $2.68 per gallon during Q1, above the company's guidance of $2.50-$2.55, due to refinery disruptions on the Gulf Coast. Non-fuel unit costs rose 3.8%, well above the 1.5% guidance, due to newly ratified labor contracts that included significant wage increases for pilots and flight attendants. CEO Ed Bastian lowered full-year 2026 EPS guidance to $6.00-$7.00 from $7.00-$8.00, a reduction that rippled across the airline sector as investors recalibrated expectations. United Airlines fell 7%, American Airlines declined 8%, and Southwest dropped 5% in sympathy. The Delta surprise raised broader concerns about whether the post-pandemic travel boom has finally run its course and whether airline profitability can be sustained in a softer demand environment with structurally higher labor costs. The price war in domestic markets, with ultra-low-cost carriers adding capacity aggressively, appears to be eroding pricing power that the legacy carriers had enjoyed for several quarters.

items.name: Supermicro (SMCI) — Server Maker Defies Doubters
items.detail: Supermicro Computer delivered a stunning positive earnings surprise that silenced — at least temporarily — the bears who had been questioning the company's accounting practices and competitive positioning. Revenue of $6.8 billion represented a 58% year-over-year increase and crushed the consensus estimate of $5.9 billion by 15.3%, the company's largest beat in at least five years. The blowout was driven by surging demand for the company's liquid-cooled server platforms optimized for NVIDIA's Blackwell GPU architecture, which CEO Charles Liang described as experiencing 'overwhelming demand' from hyperscale customers and enterprise clients. Gross margins improved to 15.8% from 13.2% in Q4 2025, a critical metric for the bears who had argued that Supermicro's discount pricing model was unsustainable. The margin expansion was attributed to improved manufacturing efficiency at the company's new Malaysia production facility, higher-margin liquid cooling solutions that now represent 28% of revenue, and a favorable product mix shift toward higher-ASP AI server configurations. Operating income surged to $512 million from $289 million in the prior quarter, and the company generated $890 million in free cash flow, a significant improvement from the cash burn that had worried investors during the 2025 audit controversy. Management raised full-year revenue guidance to $25.5 billion from $22.5 billion, implying approximately 52% growth, and announced plans to increase total manufacturing capacity by 60% through expansions in San Jose, Taiwan, and Malaysia. The stock surged 28% in the three days following the report, recovering much of the ground lost during the 2025 sell-off triggered by the delayed 10-K filing and Hindenburg Research short report. While the bears maintain that Supermicro's reliance on NVIDIA as a sole GPU supplier and its history of governance concerns present ongoing risks, the Q1 results demonstrated that the company is capturing a meaningful share of the AI server market with a differentiated liquid cooling technology that resonates with customers deploying high-density compute clusters.

items.name: Starbucks (SBUX) — Coffee Giant Gets a Jolt from Turnaround Plan
items.detail: Starbucks delivered the most surprising positive earnings beat in the consumer sector during Q1 2026, reporting adjusted earnings per share of $0.74, exceeding the consensus estimate of $0.59 by 25.4% and marking the company's largest earnings surprise in over five years. The beat was driven by a combination of improving same-store sales, meaningful cost reductions, and the early fruits of CEO Brian Niccol's ambitious turnaround plan launched in late 2025. Global same-store sales declined just 1%, a dramatic improvement from the 4% decline in Q4 2025 and far better than the 3% decline analysts had expected. In North America, same-store sales were flat year-over-year as average ticket growth of 3% offset a 3% decline in transactions — the smallest transaction decline in four quarters, suggesting that the brand's effort to improve the customer experience and reduce wait times is beginning to resonate. Niccol highlighted several initiatives that are gaining traction: the simplification of the menu to reduce complexity and speed service, the deployment of the Siren Craft System to improve barista efficiency, the elimination of surcharges for non-dairy milk alternatives, and the reintroduction of cozy store ambiance with comfortable seating and free Wi-Fi to encourage longer visits. Operating margins expanded to 15.2% from 12.8% in Q1 2025, driven by the cost optimization program that eliminated approximately $400 million in annualized expenses through supply chain improvements, labor scheduling optimization, and the closure of 150 underperforming US locations. International same-store sales grew 2%, with China showing particular improvement as the business stabilized after several quarters of intense competition from Luckin Coffee and other domestic operators. The stock surged 16% on the earnings report, its best single-day performance since November 2018, as investors signaled confidence that the Niccol-led turnaround is gaining momentum. While Starbucks still faces significant competitive and macroeconomic headwinds, the Q1 results provided tangible evidence that the strategic pivot is working, and management's full-year EPS guidance of $3.20-$3.40, up from $2.90-$3.10, suggests further improvement ahead.

items.name: Intel (INTC) — Chip Pioneer's Continued Decline Shocks Bulls
items.detail: Intel delivered the most anticipated negative earnings surprise of Q1 2026, and even the pessimists were caught off guard by the severity of the miss. Revenue of $11.2 billion declined 8% year-over-year and fell 7.3% below the consensus estimate of $12.1 billion, representing the company's fifth consecutive quarter of revenue misses. The Client Computing Group, which still accounts for approximately 54% of total revenue, declined 12% to $6.1 billion as PC demand remained sluggish and Intel continued to lose market share to AMD and Arm-based processors from Qualcomm. The Data Center and AI Group revenue of $3.8 billion was essentially flat year-over-year — a devastating result considering that NVIDIA's data center revenue grew 73% during the same period — and operating margins in the segment were negative, reflecting the massive R&D and manufacturing investments that have yet to generate meaningful commercial returns. CEO Pat Gelsinger, who has been leading Intel's ambitious IDM 2.0 transformation strategy since 2021, announced a further restructuring that includes the elimination of 8,500 positions (approximately 7% of the workforce), the cancellation of several next-generation product lines, and the acceleration of the foundry services business as the company's best path to relevance in the AI era. The foundry business generated just $320 million in revenue during Q1, far below internal targets, though management highlighted progress in securing commitments from major design customers for Intel 18A process technology. Free cash flow was negative $4.8 billion for the quarter, and the company's cash position declined to $18.2 billion from $23.5 billion at year-end 2025. Intel suspended its quarterly dividend for the second time in three years to preserve cash, a move that particularly angered income-oriented shareholders. The stock fell 13% on the report, reaching its lowest level since 2013, and several analysts downgraded the stock to sell, with price targets as low as $16 per share. The Intel surprise crystallized the dramatic shift in semiconductor industry leadership, with AI-focused companies like NVIDIA capturing virtually all of the value creation while legacy CPU-centric companies struggle to reinvent themselves.
